record_office_terminology.land_records: Recorder of Deeds (deeds, mortgages, liens -- one office per county)
record_office_terminology.probate: Register of Wills (wills, estates); Orphans Court (guardianships, minors' estates, apprenticeships)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Clerk of the Peace (marriage licenses; historically from 1744)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1861 (births/deaths registration began sporadically; statewide compliance improved after 1881; modern records from Delaware Division of Public Health)

burned_county_authority: Delaware Public Archives (DPA), Dover -- Delaware has no burned counties; record survival is excellent across all three counties due to the state's small size, stable governance, and the DPA's centralized preservation efforts
administrative_notes: Delaware has only 3 counties, making it the smallest state by county count. Originally settled by the Dutch (1631) and Swedes (1638, New Sweden colony at Fort Christina). Became English territory in 1664. Known as the 'Three Lower Counties on Delaware' under William Penn's charter, Delaware operated as part of Pennsylvania until declaring independence in 1776. First state to ratify the US Constitution on December 7, 1787. Delaware was a slave state that did NOT secede during the Civil War -- it remained a border state loyal to the Union. Slavery was not formally abolished in Delaware until the 13th Amendment (1865). The Delaware Public Archives (DPA) in Dover is the central state repository, holding records from all three counties. Delaware's counties are subdivided into 'hundreds' (an Anglo-Saxon territorial unit used for tax assessment and militia organization). Each county has a Recorder of Deeds, Register of Wills, and Clerk of the Peace. The Court of Chancery (equity court) is a uniquely important Delaware institution -- its records document estate disputes, guardianships, and property matters not found in common law courts.

core_repositories.description: Delaware's state archives and the single most important repository for genealogical research in Delaware. Holds county records (deeds, probate, orphans court, tax lists), vital records, military records, court records, legislative records, and executive records. Microfilmed county records from all three counties. Collections include colonial-era documents, slavery-related records (manumissions, free negro bonds), and state government records from 1776. Research room open to the public in Dover.

Major manuscript repository holding family papers, business records, church records, photographs, and maps. Strong collections for New Castle County and Wilmington. Houses the research library with published genealogies, county histories, and Delaware periodicals. Wilmington city directories from the 1840s. Collections related to the DuPont family, Quaker communities, and African American history.

core_repositories.repository: Hagley Museum and Library, Greenville (Wilmington area)
core_repositories.description: Specialized repository for business, industrial, and technological history. Holds DuPont company records, Brandywine Valley industrial records (powder mills, textile mills), and worker records. Employment records, company towns, and industrial accident reports document working families. Also holds personal papers of industrialist families.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Delaware county records (deeds, probate, marriages), vital records, church records, and cemetery records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at DPA and county offices. Key collections include Delaware marriages (1744-1930), probate records, and orphans court records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability guides, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Delaware's unique record types (hundreds, orphans court, Court of Chancery) and provides research strategies.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Delaware newspapers from the late 1700s. Includes Wilmington papers (Delaware Gazette, Every Evening) and county weeklies (Delaware State News, Sussex Countian). Legal notices, obituaries, estate advertisements, and community news.

Microfilmed Delaware newspapers held at DPA. County weeklies and Wilmington dailies. Researchers should check newspapers for legal notices (estate settlements, land sales, divorces), runaway slave advertisements, and vital event announcements predating civil registration.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Delaware Slavery and Free Negro Records -- DPA
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Delaware was a slave state until the 13th Amendment (1865). DPA holds manumission records (documenting emancipation of enslaved persons), free negro bonds (required for free Black persons to post financial security), slave sale bills, estate inventories listing enslaved persons by name and value, and tax assessments enumerating enslaved persons by slaveholder. Sussex County had the highest enslaved population; New Castle County had the most diverse free Black community. Kent County's free Black population grew significantly in the early 1800s through manumission.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Underground Railroad and African American Collections -- Historical Society of Delaware
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Wilmington was a critical Underground Railroad station. Thomas Garrett (Quaker abolitionist) aided over 2,700 freedom seekers. The Historical Society holds Garrett's papers, African American church records (AME churches), and documentation of Wilmington's free Black community. Essential for tracing African American families in Delaware.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Wilmington Port and Naturalization Records -- DPA / NARA Philadelphia
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Delaware Public Archives, Dover / NARA Mid-Atlantic Region, Philadelphia
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Wilmington was a significant port of entry. Naturalization records from Delaware courts (Superior Court, Court of Common Pleas) held at DPA from 1795; federal court naturalization records at NARA Philadelphia. Ship passenger lists for Wilmington port. Delaware attracted Swedish, Finnish, Dutch, English, Irish, Italian, and Polish immigrants at various periods. DuPont powder mills and Wilmington industries drew immigrant workers.

military_records.description: DPA holds state militia records, adjutant general records, and service records. Delaware Line (Continental Army) records for the Revolutionary War. War of 1812 militia musters. Civil War Union service records (Delaware remained in the Union). Fort Delaware on Pea Patch Island served as a Confederate prisoner-of-war camp -- prisoner records and death registers at NARA. WWI and WWII draft registrations.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- Delaware
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Delaware soldiers. Delaware contributed approximately 12,000 Union soldiers including the US Colored Troops regiments recruited from Delaware's African American community.

land_and_property.source_name: Delaware Land Records -- County Recorders / DPA
land_and_property.repository: Delaware Public Archives, Dover / County Recorder of Deeds offices
land_and_property.description: Each of Delaware's three counties has a Recorder of Deeds office. Land records date from the 1670s (New Castle County is earliest at 1673). DPA holds microfilm copies of historical deed books from all three counties. Colonial land grants under Penn's charter, warrants, and surveys are at DPA. Delaware used metes and bounds surveys. New Castle County Recorder has online access for recent records.

vital_records.source_name: Delaware Vital Records -- DPA / Division of Public Health
vital_records.repository: Delaware Public Archives, Dover / Delaware Division of Public Health
vital_records.description: Birth and death registration began in Delaware in 1861 but compliance was poor until the 1880s. Marriage licenses from 1744 (Clerk of the Peace). Historical vital records transferred to DPA over time. Modern vital records (recent births, deaths, marriages) from the Delaware Division of Public Health. For pre-registration vital events, researchers must use church records, cemetery records, and newspaper notices.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Delaware Church Records -- DPA / Historical Society / Swarthmore College
church_and_cemetery.repository: Delaware Public Archives / Historical Society of Delaware / Swarthmore College Friends Historical Library
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are essential for pre-civil registration research in Delaware. Holy Trinity (Old Swedes) Church, Wilmington (1698) has among the oldest continuous church records in America. Quaker (Friends) meeting records from the 1680s are at Swarthmore College. Anglican/Episcopal parish records from the 1680s. Methodist records from the late 1700s (Barratt's Chapel, 1780). Presbyterian records for Sussex County. Catholic records from the early 1800s. African Methodist Episcopal church records are critical for African American family research.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Delaware. Coverage varies by county. The Delaware Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcription volumes. Church cemeteries, family burial grounds, and municipal cemeteries are included. Wilmington and Brandywine Cemetery has notable historical burials.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society publishing the Delaware Genealogical Society Journal. Maintains surname files, cemetery transcriptions, Bible record collections, and research guides for all three counties. Library holdings include published genealogies and Delaware family histories.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all three Delaware counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, and research guides.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Delaware has no extinct counties. The three counties -- Kent, New Castle, and Sussex -- have existed continuously since the 1670s-1680s, making them among the oldest county jurisdictions in the United States. Before county organization, the area was administered as part of the New Sweden colony (1638-1655), the Dutch colony of New Netherland (1655-1664), and the English Duke of York's territory (1664-1682). Under William Penn's charter (1682), the three counties were known as the 'Three Lower Counties on Delaware' or 'Territories' and had a shared assembly with Pennsylvania until 1701, when they received their own assembly. Delaware declared independence from both Pennsylvania and Britain in 1776. The county boundaries have remained essentially unchanged for over 340 years.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Delaware has no burned counties. Record survival across all three counties is excellent. The Delaware Public Archives in Dover has centralized preservation of historical records from all three counties, including microfilm copies of deed books, probate records, and court records. The state's small size and stable governance contributed to strong record preservation. Some early Dutch and Swedish colonial records (pre-1664) are fragmentary, but English-period records from the 1670s forward are substantially complete.
